content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 19, 1990

Lowell Finley
Remcho, Johansen & Purcell
220 Montgomery Street, Ste. 800
San Francisco, CA 94104

Re: Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No. A-90-607

Dear Mr. Finley:

This is in response to your request on behalf of Assemblyman
Patrick Johnston for confirmation of telephone advice regarding
campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Mr.
Johnston is currently an assemblyman running for reelection in the
26th Assembly District. The election for that seat will be held
on November 6, 1990. Mr. Johnston is also running for election to
an open seat in the 5th Senate District. The senate seat is a
special election to fill a vacancy opened on September 4, 1990.
The date of the special election is November 6, 1990. Mr.
Johnston has opened bank accounts and has controlled committees
for each of the above seats.

Your letter, dated September 21, 1990, correctly summarizes
my advice that, during the Senate special election cycle, a
contributor may contribute up to the applicable limit to each of
the committees, without the contributions being aggregated. Thus,
during the current Senate special election cycle, an individual
who has not contributed any committee controlled by Mr.
Johnston during the current fiscal year may contribute $1,000 to
the Assembly reelection committee, and $1,000 to the Senate
special election committee. The same principles would apply to
permit a political committee to contribute up to $2,500 to each
committee, and a broad based political committee to contribute up
to $5,000 to each committee. An individual who had contributed

1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Lowell Finley
A-90-607
Page 2


$1,000 to the Assembly committee during the current fiscal year,
could contribute $1,000 during the Senate special election cycle
to the Senate committee. The same type of restriction would also
apply to contributions from political committees or broad based
political committees, although the contribution limits for those
committees differ from the limits for individuals.

Your letter also correctly summarizes my advice that in a
special runoff election for the Senate seat, a contributor who has
previously contributed to the Assembly committee and to the Senate
committee during the special election cycle, may make an additional contribution to the Senate committee, up to the applicable limit during the "special runoff election cycle." The
special runoff election cycle begins the day after the special
election and ends the day of the special election runoff
election.2

If you have additional questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Sandra L. Taylor
Political Reform Consultant


2
On September 25, 1990 the United States District Court, Eastern
District of California, in the case of Service Employees
International Union et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission,
Case No. CIVS 89-0433 LKK-JFM, ruled that some of the contribution
limits placed in the Act by Proposition 73 were unconstitutional.
The sections of the Act discussed above were unaffected by this
ruling.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether campaign contributions to committees controlled by a candidate running in both an Assembly reelection and a Senate special election can be made separately to each committee without aggregation, and how contribution limits apply in a potential Senate runoff election scenario.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: During a Senate special election cycle, contributors may give up to applicable limits to each committee separately without aggregation (e.g., $1,000 to Assembly committee and $1,000 to Senate committee for individuals). In a special runoff election, additional contributions up to applicable limits are permitted to the Senate committee, with the runoff election cycle beginning the day after the special election.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is confirmation of prior telephone advice rather than response to written request with formal Q/A sections. Conclusion information is embedded in body paragraphs rather than in separate conclusion section. No formal question or conclusion headers present.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether campaign contributions to committees controlled by a candidate running in both an Assembly reelection and a Senate special election can be made separately to each committee without aggregation, and how contribution limits apply in a potential Senate runoff election scenario.

CONCLUSION: During a Senate special election cycle, contributors may give up to applicable limits to each committee separately without aggregation (e.g., $1,000 to Assembly committee and $1,000 to Senate committee for individuals). In a special runoff election, additional contributions up to applicable limits are permitted to the Senate committee, with the runoff election cycle beginning the day after the special election.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirms telephone advice regarding Assemblyman Patrick Johnston's campaign contributions across separate committees for his concurrent Assembly reelection and Senate special election campaigns, clarifying that contributions to each committee need not be aggregated and explaining applicable limits for runoff elections.
